title: DisjointHardInterventions

lean_statement.name: hardInterventionOn_swig_comm
lean_statement.code:
/-- claim_3_11 (`DisjointHardInterventions`): for a CDMG `G : CDMG О±`
and disjoint subsets `Wв‚Ѓ Wв‚‚ : Set О±` with `Wв‚‚ вЉ† G.V`, hard intervention
on `Wв‚Ѓ` and the node-splitting hard intervention (SWIG) on `Wв‚‚`
commute. Mirrors the displayed equation in the `\Lem` at
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` line 669. -/
theorem hardInterventionOn_swig_comm
    {G : CDMG О±} {Wв‚Ѓ Wв‚‚ : Set О±}
    (hWв‚‚ : Wв‚‚ вЉ† G.V) (hdisj : Disjoint Wв‚Ѓ Wв‚‚) :
    (G.hardInterventionOn Wв‚Ѓ).swig Wв‚‚
        (subset_hardInterventionOn_V_of_disjoint hWв‚‚ hdisj)
      = (G.swig Wв‚‚ hWв‚‚).hardInterventionOn (Sum.inl '' Wв‚Ѓ) := by

lean_explanation:
The LN states an equality of CDMGs $(G_{\doit(W_1)})_{\swig(W_2)} = (G_{\swig(W_2)})_{\doit(W_1)}$ between two ways of composing a hard intervention and a node-splitting hard intervention (SWIG). The Lean encoding pins this as a literal `Eq` of CDMGs because both sides share the carrier `О± вЉ• в†‘Wв‚‚` вЂ” the carrier inherited from a single SWIG on $W_2$.

`О± : Type*` is the ambient vertex type, matching the LN's silent assumption that $G$ lives over some unspecified vertex set; `G : CDMG О±` is $G = (J, V, E, L)$; `Wв‚Ѓ Wв‚‚ : Set О±` are the two subsets. The hypothesis `hWв‚‚ : Wв‚‚ вЉ† G.V` is the LN's $W_2 \subseteq V$; `Disjoint Wв‚Ѓ Wв‚‚` is Mathlib's order-theoretic disjointness predicate, which on `Set` unfolds to $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$. The LN's third precondition $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$ does not appear as a Lean hypothesis; see the design notes.

The discharge `subset_hardInterventionOn_V_of_disjoint hWв‚‚ hdisj` packages the bridging inclusion $W_2 \subseteq V \setminus W_1$ that the LN states parenthetically. The helper itself lives in the bare-NS sibling claim_3_8's file (`HardInterventionNodeSplittingCommute.lean`), where it was introduced and explicitly kept public for this row's re-use; it discharges the precondition of the inner `swig Wв‚‚` call on the LHS, whose vertex set has already been reduced by the hard intervention to $V \setminus W_1$.

Mathlib idioms worth flagging: `Set.image` (written infix as `''`) lifts a function pointwise to sets, so `Sum.inl '' Wв‚Ѓ : Set (О± вЉ• в†‘Wв‚‚)` is the canonical embedding of $W_1$ into the post-SWIG carrier via the LN's "0-copy" identification of $\alpha$ with $\mathrm{inl}(\alpha)$. `в†‘Wв‚‚` is the coerced subtype `{x // x в€€ Wв‚‚}` вЂ” Lean's way of carrying the LN's output copies $w^o$ for $w \in W_2$. `Disjoint` comes from Mathlib's lattice API rather than a primitive set predicate, which lets it compose uniformly with the rest of the order library. The surface name `G.swig W hW` is a `noncomputable abbrev` for the long-form `nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn` (def_3_12), chosen here so the Lean statement reads as close to the LN's $G_{\swig(W)}$ notation as possible.

The theorem takes the implicit data `G Wв‚Ѓ Wв‚‚` and the two explicit hypotheses `hWв‚‚` and `hdisj`, and concludes literal `Eq` of two CDMGs over `О± вЉ• в†‘Wв‚‚`. The LHS applies `hardInterventionOn` first (carrier-preserving on `О±`) and `swig` second (extending to `О± вЉ• в†‘Wв‚‚`); the RHS applies them in the opposite order, with the LN's "the same $W_1$" on the outer HI spelled out as the lift `Sum.inl '' Wв‚Ѓ` because the outer HI now lives over the post-SWIG carrier.
design_choices:
**Literal `Eq`, not `CDMGEquiv`.** Both sides have carrier `О± вЉ• в†‘Wв‚‚`, definitionally equal across the two compositions: on the LHS `hardInterventionOn Wв‚Ѓ` is carrier-preserving (`О± в†’ О±`) and `swig Wв‚‚` then extends to `О± вЉ• в†‘Wв‚‚`; on the RHS `swig Wв‚‚` extends first to `О± вЉ• в†‘Wв‚‚` and `hardInterventionOn (Sum.inl '' Wв‚Ѓ)` then preserves that carrier. The SWIG itself inherits its carrier shape verbatim from `nodeSplittingOn` вЂ” the inner hard-intervention layer of `swig` does not touch the carrier, only deleting the `range Sum.inr` summand of the vertex set вЂ” so the two iterated carriers match the bare-NS sibling claim_3_8 exactly. No re-labeling bijection is needed and literal `Eq` of CDMGs is type-correct. This puts the row in the same regime as claim_3_4 and claim_3_8 (literal-`Eq` family). The contrast is the SWIG--SWIG sibling claim_3_10, which iterates *two* SWIGs on disjoint sets: there the carriers are `(О± вЉ• в†‘Wв‚Ѓ) вЉ• в†‘(Sum.inl '' Wв‚‚)` versus `(О± вЉ• в†‘Wв‚‚) вЉ• в†‘(Sum.inl '' Wв‚Ѓ)`, definitionally distinct, forcing `CDMGEquiv`. Here `swig` is applied only once and on the *same* set on each side, and the partner operation is the carrier-preserving `hardInterventionOn`; that absorbs the iteration without forcing a re-labeling bijection.

**No `Wв‚Ѓ вЉ† J в€Є V` precondition.** The LN writes $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$ as part of its preamble, but `hardInterventionOn` is well-defined for every `Wв‚Ѓ : Set О±`, and vertices in $W_1 \setminus (J \cup V)$ are inert: no edge has an endpoint there (by `E_subset` / `L_subset`), so removing them under hard intervention is the identity on all four CDMG data fields. They ride along the LHS = RHS equality without contributing anything, and the commute identity holds for arbitrary `Wв‚Ѓ`. The LN's $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$ is informal scaffolding to make $\doit(W_1)$ mathematically meaningful in prose вЂ” dropping it makes the lemma maximally useful at call sites without weakening it. This is the same drop made in the bare-NS sibling claim_3_8.

**Reuse the bridging helper from claim_3_8 rather than redeclaring it.** The LHS's inner `swig Wв‚‚` requires $W_2 \subseteq (G.\mathrm{hardInterventionOn}\,W_1).V = V \setminus W_1$, which follows from `hWв‚‚` together with disjointness. That small bridge is `subset_hardInterventionOn_V_of_disjoint`, introduced for claim_3_8 and explicitly kept public there with this row in mind as the anticipated second consumer. We use it verbatim. Both `hWв‚‚` and `hdisj` are therefore load-bearing for typing alone: dropping either makes the LHS fail to elaborate.

**Lift `Wв‚Ѓ в†¦ Sum.inl '' Wв‚Ѓ` on the RHS's HI argument.** The outer operation on the RHS is hard intervention on the post-SWIG graph, which lives over the carrier `О± вЉ• в†‘Wв‚‚`. Its target set must therefore be a `Set (О± вЉ• в†‘Wв‚‚)`, not a `Set О±`. Under the convention `Sum.inl = 0-copy = canonical observation copy` from def_3_11 / def_3_12, the LN's "the same $W_1$" *is* the `inl`-image of $W_1$ in the SWIG carrier; the LN identifies $\alpha \cong \mathrm{inl}(\alpha)$ implicitly throughout ("we make the identification $W = W^o$" at `graphs.tex` line 197), but Lean's stricter type discipline forces us to spell the lift out. For SWIG specifically, $V$ is just $\mathrm{Sum.inl}\,\!''\,G.V$ вЂ” the `range Sum.inr` piece that bare `nodeSplittingOn` would carry is killed by the SWIG's inner hard intervention вЂ” so removing `Sum.inl '' Wв‚Ѓ` from the post-SWIG graph mirrors "then $\doit(W_1)$" on the right-hand side exactly. The narrower lift `Sum.inl '' (Wв‚Ѓ в€© (G.J в€Є G.V))` was rejected on the same two counts as in claim_3_8: by inertness it would yield the same RHS CDMG, and it would force every call site to thread $W_1 \cap (G.J \cup G.V)$ as an extra ingredient.

**Surface API `G.swig W hW`, not the long-form `nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn`.** The four `@[simp]` characterisation lemmas for the SWIG (`nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_J`, `_V`, `mem_..._E`, `mem_..._L`) are stated about the long-form name but fire on `G.swig W hW` by reducibility, because `swig` is declared as a `noncomputable abbrev`. Choosing `swig` in the statement therefore loses nothing simp-wise and gains the LN's notation verbatim (the prose $G_{\swig(W)}$ reads as `G.swig W` in Lean).

**Argument order `hWв‚‚` before `hdisj`.** Mirrors the signature of the bare-NS sibling `hardInterventionOn_nodeSplittingOn_comm` (claim_3_8). The two `_comm` lemmas form a parallel pair, and identical hypothesis order lets a downstream consumer swap one for the other with zero call-site churn. It also matches the helper `subset_hardInterventionOn_V_of_disjoint`'s own argument order, so the bridging discharge slots in left-to-right without re-ordering.

**Naming `hardInterventionOn_swig_comm`.** Follows the Mathlib `_comm` convention for commutativity of two operators (`add_comm`, `mul_comm`, `Set.union_comm`), with both operators in the name and ordered left-to-right to match the LHS of the conclusion. The subsection's commute-lemma family now reads as a uniform menu: `_comm` for the literal-`Eq` rows (`hardInterventionOn_comm` for claim_3_4, `hardInterventionOn_nodeSplittingOn_comm` for claim_3_8, `hardInterventionOn_swig_comm` here) and `_comm_equiv` for the `CDMGEquiv` rows (`nodeSplittingOn_comm_equiv` for claim_3_7, `swig_comm_equiv` for claim_3_10). The mirror `swig_hardInterventionOn_comm` (operators reversed) is technically equivalent but not exposed вЂ” consumers can use `.symm` if they want the swap.

**Distinct from the downstream claim_3_15.** A future reader should not collapse this row with claim_3_15 ("Adding intervention nodes commutes with disjoint node-splitting hard interventions"), which states a *different* identity $(G_{\swig(W_1)})_{\doit(I_{W_2})} = (G_{\doit(I_{W_2})})_{\swig(W_1)}$, where $\doit(I_{W_2})$ is the intervention on the *intervention-node extension* of $W_2$ rather than on $W_2$ itself. claim_3_11 is the direct SWIG-mirror of claim_3_8 вЂ” foundational, not a special case or corollary of any later commute lemma вЂ” and the two displayed equations only look superficially similar.
